title: List of PostgreSQL server parameters. see https://www.postgresql.org/docs/current/runtime-config.html and https://postgresqlco.nf

allow_in_place_tablespaces.title: allow_in_place_tablespaces
allow_in_place_tablespaces.description:
Allows tablespaces directly inside pg_tblspc, for testing.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/allow_in_place_tablespaces/
allow_in_place_tablespaces.markdownDescription:
Allows tablespaces directly inside pg_tblspc, for testing.

see [allow_in_place_tablespaces documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/allow_in_place_tablespaces/)

allow_system_table_mods.title: allow_system_table_mods
allow_system_table_mods.description:
Allows modifications of the structure of system tables.

Only available in single-user mode; this setting is for initdb and may be used in the future for upgrade-in-place.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/allow_system_table_mods/
allow_system_table_mods.markdownDescription:
Allows modifications of the structure of system tables.

Only available in single-user mode; this setting is for initdb and may be used in the future for upgrade-in-place.

see [allow_system_table_mods documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/allow_system_table_mods/)

application_name.title: application_name
application_name.description:
Sets the application name to be reported in statistics and logs.

Set this to a reasonable default for most user sessions; if in the middle of working over your application to support application names, this might be "unknown".

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/application_name/
application_name.markdownDescription:
Sets the application name to be reported in statistics and logs.

Set this to a reasonable default for most user sessions; if in the middle of working over your application to support application names, this might be "unknown".

see [application_name documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/application_name/)

archive_cleanup_command.title: archive_cleanup_command
archive_cleanup_command.description:
Sets the shell command that will be executed at every restart point.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/archive_cleanup_command/
archive_cleanup_command.markdownDescription:
Sets the shell command that will be executed at every restart point.

see [archive_cleanup_command documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/archive_cleanup_command/)

archive_command.title: archive_command
archive_command.description:
Sets the shell command that will be called to archive a WAL file.

All of the Archiving settings are part of a Point In Time Recovery or Warm Standby configuration.
Please see the Backup and Restore section for more information.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/archive_command/
archive_command.markdownDescription:
Sets the shell command that will be called to archive a WAL file.

All of the Archiving settings are part of a Point In Time Recovery or Warm Standby configuration.
Please see the Backup and Restore section for more information.

see [archive_command documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/archive_command/)

archive_mode.title: archive_mode
archive_mode.description:
Allows archiving of WAL files using archive_command.

Requires a restart to change, so if you want to turn archiving on and off, set this to 'on' and change archive_command instead.
Even better, set archive_command to a script which can be disabled by trigger or ENV variable.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/archive_mode/
archive_mode.markdownDescription:
Allows archiving of WAL files using archive_command.

Requires a restart to change, so if you want to turn archiving on and off, set this to 'on' and change archive_command instead.
Even better, set archive_command to a script which can be disabled by trigger or ENV variable.

see [archive_mode documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/archive_mode/)

archive_timeout.title: archive_timeout
archive_timeout.description:
Forces a switch to the next WAL file if a new file has not been started within N seconds.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1073741823

Dependant on your tradeoff between disk space and letting the standby get behind.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/archive_timeout/
archive_timeout.markdownDescription:
Forces a switch to the next WAL file if a new file has not been started within N seconds.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1073741823

Dependant on your tradeoff between disk space and letting the standby get behind.

see [archive_timeout documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/archive_timeout/)

array_nulls.title: array_nulls
array_nulls.description:
Enable input of NULL elements in arrays.

Provided for compatibility with 7.4 behavior.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/array_nulls/
array_nulls.markdownDescription:
Enable input of NULL elements in arrays.

Provided for compatibility with 7.4 behavior.

see [array_nulls documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/array_nulls/)

authentication_timeout.title: authentication_timeout
authentication_timeout.description:
Sets the maximum allowed time to complete client authentication.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 600

For production databases, it's important that this value be synchronized with the timeout on the application server side.
Most web applications will want a shorter timeout, like 20s.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/authentication_timeout/
authentication_timeout.markdownDescription:
Sets the maximum allowed time to complete client authentication.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 600

For production databases, it's important that this value be synchronized with the timeout on the application server side.
Most web applications will want a shorter timeout, like 20s.

see [authentication_timeout documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/authentication_timeout/)

autovacuum.title: autovacuum
autovacuum.description:
Starts the autovacuum subprocess.

Starts the daemon which cleans up your tables and indexes, preventing bloat and poor response times. The only reason to set it to "off" is for databases which regularly do large batch operations like ETL.
Note that you can adjust the frequency or stop autovacuum on individual tables by adding rows to the pg_autovacuum system table.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum/
autovacuum.markdownDescription:
Starts the autovacuum subprocess.

Starts the daemon which cleans up your tables and indexes, preventing bloat and poor response times. The only reason to set it to "off" is for databases which regularly do large batch operations like ETL.
Note that you can adjust the frequency or stop autovacuum on individual tables by adding rows to the pg_autovacuum system table.

see [autovacuum documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum/)

autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor.title: autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor
autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor.description:
Number of tuple inserts, updates, or deletes prior to analyze as a fraction of reltuples.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 100

This setting should be optimal for most databases.
However, very large tables (1m rows or more) in which rows are added in a skewed fashion may need to be autoanalyzed at a lower percentage, such as 5% or even 1%.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor/
autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor.markdownDescription:
Number of tuple inserts, updates, or deletes prior to analyze as a fraction of reltuples.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 100

This setting should be optimal for most databases.
However, very large tables (1m rows or more) in which rows are added in a skewed fashion may need to be autoanalyzed at a lower percentage, such as 5% or even 1%.

see [autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor/)

autovacuum_analyze_threshold.title: autovacuum_analyze_threshold
autovacuum_analyze_threshold.description:
Minimum number of tuple inserts, updates, or deletes prior to analyze.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_analyze_threshold/
autovacuum_analyze_threshold.markdownDescription:
Minimum number of tuple inserts, updates, or deletes prior to analyze.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see [autovacuum_analyze_threshold documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_analyze_threshold/)

autovacuum_freeze_max_age.title: autovacuum_freeze_max_age
autovacuum_freeze_max_age.description:
Age at which to autovacuum a table to prevent transaction ID wraparound.

Minimum: 100000

Maximum: 2000000000

Triggers autovacuum automatically if a table is about to suffer from XID rollover. The setting is very conservative, and should probably be increased to 500million, but not higher.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_freeze_max_age/
autovacuum_freeze_max_age.markdownDescription:
Age at which to autovacuum a table to prevent transaction ID wraparound.

Minimum: 100000

Maximum: 2000000000

Triggers autovacuum automatically if a table is about to suffer from XID rollover. The setting is very conservative, and should probably be increased to 500million, but not higher.

see [autovacuum_freeze_max_age documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_freeze_max_age/)

autovacuum_max_workers.title: autovacuum_max_workers
autovacuum_max_workers.description:
Sets the maximum number of simultaneously running autovacuum worker processes.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 262143

If you have an installation with many tables (100's to 1000's) or with some tables which autovacuum takes hours to process, you may want to add additional autovacuum workers so that multiple tables can be vacuumed at once.
Be conservative, though, as each autovacuum worker will utilize a separate CPU core, memory and I/O.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_max_workers/
autovacuum_max_workers.markdownDescription:
Sets the maximum number of simultaneously running autovacuum worker processes.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 262143

If you have an installation with many tables (100's to 1000's) or with some tables which autovacuum takes hours to process, you may want to add additional autovacuum workers so that multiple tables can be vacuumed at once.
Be conservative, though, as each autovacuum worker will utilize a separate CPU core, memory and I/O.

see [autovacuum_max_workers documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_max_workers/)

autovacuum_multixact_freeze_max_age.title: autovacuum_multixact_freeze_max_age
autovacuum_multixact_freeze_max_age.description:
Multixact age at which to autovacuum a table to prevent multixact wraparound.

Minimum: 10000

Maximum: 2000000000

Triggers autovacuum automatically when the oldest "multixact" (a kind of lock transaction) is more than this old.
Do not raise past 1billion.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_multixact_freeze_max_age/
autovacuum_multixact_freeze_max_age.markdownDescription:
Multixact age at which to autovacuum a table to prevent multixact wraparound.

Minimum: 10000

Maximum: 2000000000

Triggers autovacuum automatically when the oldest "multixact" (a kind of lock transaction) is more than this old.
Do not raise past 1billion.

see [autovacuum_multixact_freeze_max_age documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_multixact_freeze_max_age/)

autovacuum_naptime.title: autovacuum_naptime
autovacuum_naptime.description:
Time to sleep between autovacuum runs.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 2147483

Decrease this to 30s or 15s if you have a large number (100's) of tables, or if you otherwise see from pg_stat_user_tables that autovacuum is not keeping up.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_naptime/
autovacuum_naptime.markdownDescription:
Time to sleep between autovacuum runs.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 2147483

Decrease this to 30s or 15s if you have a large number (100's) of tables, or if you otherwise see from pg_stat_user_tables that autovacuum is not keeping up.

see [autovacuum_naptime documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_naptime/)

autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay.title: autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay
autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay.description:
Vacuum cost delay in milliseconds, for autovacuum.

Minimum: -1

Maximum: 100

If autovacuum is having too much of a performance impact on running queries, you might want to increase this setting to 50ms.
However, this will also cause individual vacuum tasks to take longer.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay/
autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay.markdownDescription:
Vacuum cost delay in milliseconds, for autovacuum.

Minimum: -1

Maximum: 100

If autovacuum is having too much of a performance impact on running queries, you might want to increase this setting to 50ms.
However, this will also cause individual vacuum tasks to take longer.

see [autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_cost_delay/)

autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit.title: autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit
autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit.description:
Vacuum cost amount available before napping, for autovacuum.

Minimum: -1

Maximum: 10000

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit/
autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit.markdownDescription:
Vacuum cost amount available before napping, for autovacuum.

Minimum: -1

Maximum: 10000

see [autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_cost_limit/)

autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor.title: autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor
autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor.description:
Number of tuple inserts prior to vacuum as a fraction of reltuples.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 100

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor/
autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor.markdownDescription:
Number of tuple inserts prior to vacuum as a fraction of reltuples.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 100

see [autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_insert_scale_factor/)

autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold.title: autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold
autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold.description:
Minimum number of tuple inserts prior to vacuum, or -1 to disable insert vacuums.

Minimum: -1

Maximum: 2147483647

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold/
autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold.markdownDescription:
Minimum number of tuple inserts prior to vacuum, or -1 to disable insert vacuums.

Minimum: -1

Maximum: 2147483647

see [autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_insert_threshold/)

autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor.title: autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor
autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor.description:
Number of tuple updates or deletes prior to vacuum as a fraction of reltuples.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 100

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor/
autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor.markdownDescription:
Number of tuple updates or deletes prior to vacuum as a fraction of reltuples.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 100

see [autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor/)

autovacuum_vacuum_threshold.title: autovacuum_vacuum_threshold
autovacuum_vacuum_threshold.description:
Minimum number of tuple updates or deletes prior to vacuum.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_threshold/
autovacuum_vacuum_threshold.markdownDescription:
Minimum number of tuple updates or deletes prior to vacuum.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see [autovacuum_vacuum_threshold documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_vacuum_threshold/)

autovacuum_work_mem.title: autovacuum_work_mem
autovacuum_work_mem.description:
Sets the maximum memory to be used by each autovacuum worker process.

Minimum: -1

Maximum: 2147483647

Set a limit on this which is based on the number of autovac workers you expect to have running.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_work_mem/
autovacuum_work_mem.markdownDescription:
Sets the maximum memory to be used by each autovacuum worker process.

Minimum: -1

Maximum: 2147483647

Set a limit on this which is based on the number of autovac workers you expect to have running.

see [autovacuum_work_mem documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/autovacuum_work_mem/)

backend_flush_after.title: backend_flush_after
backend_flush_after.description:
Number of pages after which previously performed writes are flushed to disk.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 256

Unless you have time to tune memory flushing behavior and test for improvements/regressions

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/backend_flush_after/
backend_flush_after.markdownDescription:
Number of pages after which previously performed writes are flushed to disk.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 256

Unless you have time to tune memory flushing behavior and test for improvements/regressions

see [backend_flush_after documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/backend_flush_after/)

backslash_quote.title: backslash_quote
backslash_quote.description:
Sets whether "'" is allowed in string literals.

If you have cleaned up your application code, you can set this to 'off' to help lock down the database.
Older PHP applications will require the insecure setting of 'on'.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/backslash_quote/
backslash_quote.markdownDescription:
Sets whether "'" is allowed in string literals.

If you have cleaned up your application code, you can set this to 'off' to help lock down the database.
Older PHP applications will require the insecure setting of 'on'.

see [backslash_quote documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/backslash_quote/)

backtrace_functions.title: backtrace_functions
backtrace_functions.description:
Log backtrace for errors in these functions.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/backtrace_functions/
backtrace_functions.markdownDescription:
Log backtrace for errors in these functions.

see [backtrace_functions documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/backtrace_functions/)

bgwriter_delay.title: bgwriter_delay
bgwriter_delay.description:
Background writer sleep time between rounds.

Minimum: 10

Maximum: 10000

Thanks to bgwriter autotuning, it should no longer be necessary for most users to touch the bgwriter settings.
Only modify these if you have a demonstrated issue shown by checkpoint spikes and monitoring pg_stat_bgwriter.  Laptop PostgreSQL users may want to increase bgwriter_delay to 60s to decrease I/O activity, since it is no longer possible to turn the bgwriter off.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bgwriter_delay/
bgwriter_delay.markdownDescription:
Background writer sleep time between rounds.

Minimum: 10

Maximum: 10000

Thanks to bgwriter autotuning, it should no longer be necessary for most users to touch the bgwriter settings.
Only modify these if you have a demonstrated issue shown by checkpoint spikes and monitoring pg_stat_bgwriter.  Laptop PostgreSQL users may want to increase bgwriter_delay to 60s to decrease I/O activity, since it is no longer possible to turn the bgwriter off.

see [bgwriter_delay documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bgwriter_delay/)

bgwriter_flush_after.title: bgwriter_flush_after
bgwriter_flush_after.description:
Number of pages after which previously performed writes are flushed to disk.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 256

Unless you have time to tune memory flushing behavior and test for improvements/regressions

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bgwriter_flush_after/
bgwriter_flush_after.markdownDescription:
Number of pages after which previously performed writes are flushed to disk.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 256

Unless you have time to tune memory flushing behavior and test for improvements/regressions

see [bgwriter_flush_after documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bgwriter_flush_after/)

bgwriter_lru_maxpages.title: bgwriter_lru_maxpages
bgwriter_lru_maxpages.description:
Background writer maximum number of LRU pages to flush per round.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1073741823

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bgwriter_lru_maxpages/
bgwriter_lru_maxpages.markdownDescription:
Background writer maximum number of LRU pages to flush per round.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1073741823

see [bgwriter_lru_maxpages documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bgwriter_lru_maxpages/)

bgwriter_lru_multiplier.title: bgwriter_lru_multiplier
bgwriter_lru_multiplier.description:
Multiple of the average buffer usage to free per round.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 10

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bgwriter_lru_multiplier/
bgwriter_lru_multiplier.markdownDescription:
Multiple of the average buffer usage to free per round.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 10

see [bgwriter_lru_multiplier documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bgwriter_lru_multiplier/)

block_size.title: block_size
block_size.description:
Shows the size of a disk block.

Minimum: 8192

Maximum: 8192

Informational: lets you know of non-standard installation or compile options.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/block_size/
block_size.markdownDescription:
Shows the size of a disk block.

Minimum: 8192

Maximum: 8192

Informational: lets you know of non-standard installation or compile options.

see [block_size documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/block_size/)

bonjour.title: bonjour
bonjour.description:
Enables advertising the server via Bonjour.

Set to "on" if you've compiled in Bonjour support and have an application which works by autodiscovery of Postgres.
Otherwise, leave off.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bonjour/
bonjour.markdownDescription:
Enables advertising the server via Bonjour.

Set to "on" if you've compiled in Bonjour support and have an application which works by autodiscovery of Postgres.
Otherwise, leave off.

see [bonjour documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bonjour/)

bonjour_name.title: bonjour_name
bonjour_name.description:
Sets the Bonjour service name.

Bonjour support must be compiled in and activated on the host machine to be live.
You'll want alternate names if you have several instances of PostgreSQL on the same machine.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bonjour_name/
bonjour_name.markdownDescription:
Sets the Bonjour service name.

Bonjour support must be compiled in and activated on the host machine to be live.
You'll want alternate names if you have several instances of PostgreSQL on the same machine.

see [bonjour_name documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bonjour_name/)

bytea_output.title: bytea_output
bytea_output.description:
Sets the output format for bytea.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bytea_output/
bytea_output.markdownDescription:
Sets the output format for bytea.

see [bytea_output documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/bytea_output/)

check_function_bodies.title: check_function_bodies
check_function_bodies.description:
Check routine bodies during CREATE FUNCTION and CREATE PROCEDURE.

You only really want to turn this off to resolve circular dependancies, and that can be done on a per-session basis.
In general, checking for syntax errors in PL/pgSQL functions is a very good idea.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/check_function_bodies/
check_function_bodies.markdownDescription:
Check routine bodies during CREATE FUNCTION and CREATE PROCEDURE.

You only really want to turn this off to resolve circular dependancies, and that can be done on a per-session basis.
In general, checking for syntax errors in PL/pgSQL functions is a very good idea.

see [check_function_bodies documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/check_function_bodies/)

checkpoint_completion_target.title: checkpoint_completion_target
checkpoint_completion_target.description:
Time spent flushing dirty buffers during checkpoint, as fraction of checkpoint interval.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1

Defines the fraction of one checkpoint_interval over which to spread checkpoints. The default value works for most users.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/checkpoint_completion_target/
checkpoint_completion_target.markdownDescription:
Time spent flushing dirty buffers during checkpoint, as fraction of checkpoint interval.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1

Defines the fraction of one checkpoint_interval over which to spread checkpoints. The default value works for most users.

see [checkpoint_completion_target documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/checkpoint_completion_target/)

checkpoint_flush_after.title: checkpoint_flush_after
checkpoint_flush_after.description:
Number of pages after which previously performed writes are flushed to disk.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 256

Unless you have time to tune memory flushing behavior and test for improvements/regressions

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/checkpoint_flush_after/
checkpoint_flush_after.markdownDescription:
Number of pages after which previously performed writes are flushed to disk.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 256

Unless you have time to tune memory flushing behavior and test for improvements/regressions

see [checkpoint_flush_after documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/checkpoint_flush_after/)

checkpoint_timeout.title: checkpoint_timeout
checkpoint_timeout.description:
Sets the maximum time between automatic WAL checkpoints.

Minimum: 30

Maximum: 86400

If you do really large ETL batches, you may want to increase this setting to the maximum length of a batch run.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/checkpoint_timeout/
checkpoint_timeout.markdownDescription:
Sets the maximum time between automatic WAL checkpoints.

Minimum: 30

Maximum: 86400

If you do really large ETL batches, you may want to increase this setting to the maximum length of a batch run.

see [checkpoint_timeout documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/checkpoint_timeout/)

checkpoint_warning.title: checkpoint_warning
checkpoint_warning.description:
Enables warnings if checkpoint segments are filled more frequently than this.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/checkpoint_warning/
checkpoint_warning.markdownDescription:
Enables warnings if checkpoint segments are filled more frequently than this.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see [checkpoint_warning documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/checkpoint_warning/)

client_connection_check_interval.title: client_connection_check_interval
client_connection_check_interval.description:
Sets the time interval between checks for disconnection while running queries.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/client_connection_check_interval/
client_connection_check_interval.markdownDescription:
Sets the time interval between checks for disconnection while running queries.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see [client_connection_check_interval documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/client_connection_check_interval/)

client_encoding.title: client_encoding
client_encoding.description:
Sets the client's character set encoding.

Should match server_encoding unless you have a really good reason why not.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/client_encoding/
client_encoding.markdownDescription:
Sets the client's character set encoding.

Should match server_encoding unless you have a really good reason why not.

see [client_encoding documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/client_encoding/)

client_min_messages.title: client_min_messages
client_min_messages.description:
Sets the message levels that are sent to the client.

Unless doing interactive debugging, then you want it set to DEBUG1-5.
If you have a client application which is confused by some of PostgreSQL's WARNINGs then you may want to set this to ERROR.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/client_min_messages/
client_min_messages.markdownDescription:
Sets the message levels that are sent to the client.

Unless doing interactive debugging, then you want it set to DEBUG1-5.
If you have a client application which is confused by some of PostgreSQL's WARNINGs then you may want to set this to ERROR.

see [client_min_messages documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/client_min_messages/)

cluster_name.title: cluster_name
cluster_name.description:
Sets the name of the cluster, which is included in the process title.

Should be "postgres-1" or something else identifiable as this specific postmaster.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/cluster_name/
cluster_name.markdownDescription:
Sets the name of the cluster, which is included in the process title.

Should be "postgres-1" or something else identifiable as this specific postmaster.

see [cluster_name documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/cluster_name/)

commit_delay.title: commit_delay
commit_delay.description:
Sets the delay in microseconds between transaction commit and flushing WAL to disk.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 100000

A primitive form of group commit without asynchronicity.
Performance testing of this is very mixed; only set to non-zero if you have time to test the specific performance impact on your workload.  Reasonable values are 200 to 1000.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/commit_delay/
commit_delay.markdownDescription:
Sets the delay in microseconds between transaction commit and flushing WAL to disk.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 100000

A primitive form of group commit without asynchronicity.
Performance testing of this is very mixed; only set to non-zero if you have time to test the specific performance impact on your workload.  Reasonable values are 200 to 1000.

see [commit_delay documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/commit_delay/)

commit_siblings.title: commit_siblings
commit_siblings.description:
Sets the minimum concurrent open transactions before performing commit_delay.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1000

See commit_delay.
Reasonable values are 3 to 8

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/commit_siblings/
commit_siblings.markdownDescription:
Sets the minimum concurrent open transactions before performing commit_delay.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1000

See commit_delay.
Reasonable values are 3 to 8

see [commit_siblings documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/commit_siblings/)

config_file.title: config_file
config_file.description:
Sets the server's main configuration file.

Can only be changed via command-line switch for obvious reasons.
Useful primarily for testing different configuration options, or for automated restart with different configuration options.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/config_file/
config_file.markdownDescription:
Sets the server's main configuration file.

Can only be changed via command-line switch for obvious reasons.
Useful primarily for testing different configuration options, or for automated restart with different configuration options.

see [config_file documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/config_file/)

constraint_exclusion.title: constraint_exclusion
constraint_exclusion.description:
Enables the planner to use constraints to optimize queries.

Default of "partition" is fine for most users.
Setting it to "on" can allow optimization of UNION queries as well, but deserves testing before production deployment.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/constraint_exclusion/
constraint_exclusion.markdownDescription:
Enables the planner to use constraints to optimize queries.

Default of "partition" is fine for most users.
Setting it to "on" can allow optimization of UNION queries as well, but deserves testing before production deployment.

see [constraint_exclusion documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/constraint_exclusion/)

cpu_index_tuple_cost.title: cpu_index_tuple_cost
cpu_index_tuple_cost.description:
Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of processing each index entry during an index scan.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1.79769e+308

Decrease this slightly to make your database favor indexes slightly more.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/cpu_index_tuple_cost/
cpu_index_tuple_cost.markdownDescription:
Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of processing each index entry during an index scan.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1.79769e+308

Decrease this slightly to make your database favor indexes slightly more.

see [cpu_index_tuple_cost documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/cpu_index_tuple_cost/)

cpu_operator_cost.title: cpu_operator_cost
cpu_operator_cost.description:
Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of processing each operator or function call.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1.79769e+308

Decrease this slightly to make your database favor indexes slightly more.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/cpu_operator_cost/
cpu_operator_cost.markdownDescription:
Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of processing each operator or function call.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1.79769e+308

Decrease this slightly to make your database favor indexes slightly more.

see [cpu_operator_cost documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/cpu_operator_cost/)

cpu_tuple_cost.title: cpu_tuple_cost
cpu_tuple_cost.description:
Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of processing each tuple (row).

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1.79769e+308

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/cpu_tuple_cost/
cpu_tuple_cost.markdownDescription:
Sets the planner's estimate of the cost of processing each tuple (row).

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1.79769e+308

see [cpu_tuple_cost documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/cpu_tuple_cost/)

cursor_tuple_fraction.title: cursor_tuple_fraction
cursor_tuple_fraction.description:
Sets the planner's estimate of the fraction of a cursor's rows that will be retrieved.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1

Increase this to 0.9 if most of the time you're using cursors to step through all of the rows of a query result.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/cursor_tuple_fraction/
cursor_tuple_fraction.markdownDescription:
Sets the planner's estimate of the fraction of a cursor's rows that will be retrieved.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1

Increase this to 0.9 if most of the time you're using cursors to step through all of the rows of a query result.

see [cursor_tuple_fraction documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/cursor_tuple_fraction/)

data_checksums.title: data_checksums
data_checksums.description:
Shows whether data checksums are turned on for this cluster.

This has to be set at initdb time, and does create a significant amount of extra I/O.
However, it will save you from a corrupt database down the line, so if you're not performance-constrained, always use it.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/data_checksums/
data_checksums.markdownDescription:
Shows whether data checksums are turned on for this cluster.

This has to be set at initdb time, and does create a significant amount of extra I/O.
However, it will save you from a corrupt database down the line, so if you're not performance-constrained, always use it.

see [data_checksums documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/data_checksums/)

data_directory.title: data_directory
data_directory.description:
Sets the server's data directory.

Supports the ability to distribute files according to sysadmin or operating system defined schemes, or for launching multiple restart instances using the same binaries.
Most of the time, it's better to use configuration options to define these locations so that all PostgreSQL binaries default to the correct paths.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/data_directory/
data_directory.markdownDescription:
Sets the server's data directory.

Supports the ability to distribute files according to sysadmin or operating system defined schemes, or for launching multiple restart instances using the same binaries.
Most of the time, it's better to use configuration options to define these locations so that all PostgreSQL binaries default to the correct paths.

see [data_directory documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/data_directory/)

data_directory_mode.title: data_directory_mode
data_directory_mode.description:
Shows the mode of the data directory.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 511

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/data_directory_mode/
data_directory_mode.markdownDescription:
Shows the mode of the data directory.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 511

see [data_directory_mode documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/data_directory_mode/)

data_sync_retry.title: data_sync_retry
data_sync_retry.description:
Whether to continue running after a failure to sync data files.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/data_sync_retry/
data_sync_retry.markdownDescription:
Whether to continue running after a failure to sync data files.

see [data_sync_retry documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/data_sync_retry/)

DateStyle.title: DateStyle
DateStyle.description:
Sets the display format for date and time values.

Should be set according to the format in which you expect to receive date information.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/DateStyle/
DateStyle.markdownDescription:
Sets the display format for date and time values.

Should be set according to the format in which you expect to receive date information.

see [DateStyle documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/DateStyle/)

db_user_namespace.title: db_user_namespace
db_user_namespace.description:
Enables per-database user names.

This setting is a hack to work around the lack of per-database users in PostgreSQL.
Unless you desperately need it, avoid this setting as it will eventually be replaced by something more maintainable.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/db_user_namespace/
db_user_namespace.markdownDescription:
Enables per-database user names.

This setting is a hack to work around the lack of per-database users in PostgreSQL.
Unless you desperately need it, avoid this setting as it will eventually be replaced by something more maintainable.

see [db_user_namespace documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/db_user_namespace/)

deadlock_timeout.title: deadlock_timeout
deadlock_timeout.description:
Sets the time to wait on a lock before checking for deadlock.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 2147483647

Default is fine, except when you are troubleshooting/monitoring locks.
In that case, you may want to lower it to as little as 50ms.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/deadlock_timeout/
deadlock_timeout.markdownDescription:
Sets the time to wait on a lock before checking for deadlock.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 2147483647

Default is fine, except when you are troubleshooting/monitoring locks.
In that case, you may want to lower it to as little as 50ms.

see [deadlock_timeout documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/deadlock_timeout/)

debug_assertions.title: debug_assertions
debug_assertions.description:
Shows whether the running server has assertion checks enabled.

Used for debugging PostgreSQL code problems; not for production use.
Requires compile options.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_assertions/
debug_assertions.markdownDescription:
Shows whether the running server has assertion checks enabled.

Used for debugging PostgreSQL code problems; not for production use.
Requires compile options.

see [debug_assertions documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_assertions/)

debug_discard_caches.title: debug_discard_caches
debug_discard_caches.description:
Aggressively flush system caches for debugging purposes.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 0

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_discard_caches/
debug_discard_caches.markdownDescription:
Aggressively flush system caches for debugging purposes.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 0

see [debug_discard_caches documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_discard_caches/)

debug_pretty_print.title: debug_pretty_print
debug_pretty_print.description:
Indents parse and plan tree displays.

For debugging a testing machine.
Do not set in production.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_pretty_print/
debug_pretty_print.markdownDescription:
Indents parse and plan tree displays.

For debugging a testing machine.
Do not set in production.

see [debug_pretty_print documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_pretty_print/)

debug_print_parse.title: debug_print_parse
debug_print_parse.description:
Logs each query's parse tree.

For debugging a testing machine.
Do not set in production.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_print_parse/
debug_print_parse.markdownDescription:
Logs each query's parse tree.

For debugging a testing machine.
Do not set in production.

see [debug_print_parse documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_print_parse/)

debug_print_plan.title: debug_print_plan
debug_print_plan.description:

For debugging a testing machine.
Do not set in production.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_print_plan/
debug_print_plan.markdownDescription:

Do not set in production.

see [debug_print_plan documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_print_plan/)

debug_print_rewritten.title: debug_print_rewritten
debug_print_rewritten.description:
Logs each query's rewritten parse tree.

For debugging a testing machine.
Do not set in production.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_print_rewritten/
debug_print_rewritten.markdownDescription:
Logs each query's rewritten parse tree.

For debugging a testing machine.
Do not set in production.

see [debug_print_rewritten documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/debug_print_rewritten/)

default_statistics_target.title: default_statistics_target
default_statistics_target.description:
Sets the default statistics target.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 10000

Most applications can use the default of 100.
  For very small/simple databases, decrease to 10 or 50.  Data warehousing applications generally need to use 500 to 1000.  Otherwise, increase statistics targets on a per-column basis.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_statistics_target/
default_statistics_target.markdownDescription:
Sets the default statistics target.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 10000

Most applications can use the default of 100.
  For very small/simple databases, decrease to 10 or 50.  Data warehousing applications generally need to use 500 to 1000.  Otherwise, increase statistics targets on a per-column basis.

see [default_statistics_target documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_statistics_target/)

default_table_access_method.title: default_table_access_method
default_table_access_method.description:
Sets the default table access method for new tables.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_table_access_method/
default_table_access_method.markdownDescription:
Sets the default table access method for new tables.

see [default_table_access_method documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_table_access_method/)

default_tablespace.title: default_tablespace
default_tablespace.description:
Sets the default tablespace to create tables and indexes in.

Change this if you want a different tablespace for user-created tables.
Generally, better set on a ROLE or session basis.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_tablespace/
default_tablespace.markdownDescription:
Sets the default tablespace to create tables and indexes in.

Change this if you want a different tablespace for user-created tables.
Generally, better set on a ROLE or session basis.

see [default_tablespace documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_tablespace/)

default_text_search_config.title: default_text_search_config
default_text_search_config.description:
Sets default text search configuration.

Set to the most common language used by the users, so that they don't have to pass the language parameter when calling TSearch functions.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_text_search_config/
default_text_search_config.markdownDescription:
Sets default text search configuration.

Set to the most common language used by the users, so that they don't have to pass the language parameter when calling TSearch functions.

see [default_text_search_config documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_text_search_config/)

default_toast_compression.title: default_toast_compression
default_toast_compression.description:
Sets the default compression method for compressible values.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_toast_compression/
default_toast_compression.markdownDescription:
Sets the default compression method for compressible values.

see [default_toast_compression documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_toast_compression/)

default_transaction_deferrable.title: default_transaction_deferrable
default_transaction_deferrable.description:
Sets the default deferrable status of new transactions.

If you use serializable transactions by default, it may be also useful to set this in order to decrease the overhead of long-running transactions.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_transaction_deferrable/
default_transaction_deferrable.markdownDescription:
Sets the default deferrable status of new transactions.

If you use serializable transactions by default, it may be also useful to set this in order to decrease the overhead of long-running transactions.

see [default_transaction_deferrable documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_transaction_deferrable/)

default_transaction_isolation.title: default_transaction_isolation
default_transaction_isolation.description:
Sets the transaction isolation level of each new transaction.

Relates to transaction_isolation.
Better set on a session or transaction basis as transaction_isolation in order to support specific types of transaction conflict resolution.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_transaction_isolation/
default_transaction_isolation.markdownDescription:
Sets the transaction isolation level of each new transaction.

Relates to transaction_isolation.
Better set on a session or transaction basis as transaction_isolation in order to support specific types of transaction conflict resolution.

see [default_transaction_isolation documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_transaction_isolation/)

default_transaction_read_only.title: default_transaction_read_only
default_transaction_read_only.description:
Sets the default read-only status of new transactions.

This setting is mainly useful for preventing yourself from accidentally changing data.
It is not really a security setting, as anyone can revoke it on their own session.  Better set on a session or ROLE level.  Will show up as TRUE if you are on a replication standby.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_transaction_read_only/
default_transaction_read_only.markdownDescription:
Sets the default read-only status of new transactions.

This setting is mainly useful for preventing yourself from accidentally changing data.
It is not really a security setting, as anyone can revoke it on their own session.  Better set on a session or ROLE level.  Will show up as TRUE if you are on a replication standby.

see [default_transaction_read_only documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/default_transaction_read_only/)

dynamic_library_path.title: dynamic_library_path
dynamic_library_path.description:
Sets the path for dynamically loadable modules.

Primarily useful if you've written lots of custom C libraries for your installation and want to organize them into custom directories.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/dynamic_library_path/
dynamic_library_path.markdownDescription:
Sets the path for dynamically loadable modules.

Primarily useful if you've written lots of custom C libraries for your installation and want to organize them into custom directories.

see [dynamic_library_path documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/dynamic_library_path/)

dynamic_shared_memory_type.title: dynamic_shared_memory_type
dynamic_shared_memory_type.description:
Selects the dynamic shared memory implementation used.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/dynamic_shared_memory_type/
dynamic_shared_memory_type.markdownDescription:
Selects the dynamic shared memory implementation used.

see [dynamic_shared_memory_type documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/dynamic_shared_memory_type/)

effective_cache_size.title: effective_cache_size
effective_cache_size.description:
Sets the planner's assumption about the total size of the data caches.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 2147483647

Tells the PostgreSQL query planner how much RAM is estimated to be available for caching data, in both shared_buffers and in the filesystem cache. This setting just helps the planner make good cost estimates; it does not actually allocate the memory.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/effective_cache_size/
effective_cache_size.markdownDescription:
Sets the planner's assumption about the total size of the data caches.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 2147483647

Tells the PostgreSQL query planner how much RAM is estimated to be available for caching data, in both shared_buffers and in the filesystem cache. This setting just helps the planner make good cost estimates; it does not actually allocate the memory.

see [effective_cache_size documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/effective_cache_size/)

effective_io_concurrency.title: effective_io_concurrency
effective_io_concurrency.description:
Number of simultaneous requests that can be handled efficiently by the disk subsystem.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1000

Set to the number of disks in your RAID array or number of I/O channels.
Available only for platforms with posix_fadvise support (i.e. Linux).  Currently only affects the execution of parallel bitmapscan, but might affect other I/O operations in future versions.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/effective_io_concurrency/
effective_io_concurrency.markdownDescription:
Number of simultaneous requests that can be handled efficiently by the disk subsystem.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1000

Set to the number of disks in your RAID array or number of I/O channels.
Available only for platforms with posix_fadvise support (i.e. Linux).  Currently only affects the execution of parallel bitmapscan, but might affect other I/O operations in future versions.

see [effective_io_concurrency documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/effective_io_concurrency/)

enable_async_append.title: enable_async_append
enable_async_append.description:

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_async_append/
enable_async_append.markdownDescription:

enable_bitmapscan.title: enable_bitmapscan

enable_bitmapscan.markdownDescription:

enable_parallel_hash.title: enable_parallel_hash
enable_parallel_hash.description:
Enables the planner's use of parallel hash plans.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_parallel_hash/
enable_parallel_hash.markdownDescription:
Enables the planner's use of parallel hash plans.

see [enable_parallel_hash documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_parallel_hash/)

enable_partition_pruning.title: enable_partition_pruning
enable_partition_pruning.description:
Enables plan-time and execution-time partition pruning.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_partition_pruning/
enable_partition_pruning.markdownDescription:
Enables plan-time and execution-time partition pruning.

see [enable_partition_pruning documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_partition_pruning/)

enable_partitionwise_aggregate.title: enable_partitionwise_aggregate
enable_partitionwise_aggregate.description:
Enables partitionwise aggregation and grouping.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_partitionwise_aggregate/
enable_partitionwise_aggregate.markdownDescription:
Enables partitionwise aggregation and grouping.

see [enable_partitionwise_aggregate documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_partitionwise_aggregate/)

enable_seqscan.title: enable_seqscan
enable_seqscan.description:
Enables the planner's use of sequential-scan plans.

For interactive session use only when troubleshooting queries.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_seqscan/
enable_seqscan.markdownDescription:
Enables the planner's use of sequential-scan plans.

For interactive session use only when troubleshooting queries.

see [enable_seqscan documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_seqscan/)

enable_sort.title: enable_sort
enable_sort.description:
Enables the planner's use of explicit sort steps.

For interactive session use only when troubleshooting queries.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_sort/
enable_sort.markdownDescription:
Enables the planner's use of explicit sort steps.

For interactive session use only when troubleshooting queries.

see [enable_sort documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_sort/)

enable_tidscan.title: enable_tidscan
enable_tidscan.description:
Enables the planner's use of TID scan plans.

For interactive session use only when troubleshooting queries.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_tidscan/
enable_tidscan.markdownDescription:
Enables the planner's use of TID scan plans.

see [enable_tidscan documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/enable_tidscan/)

escape_string_warning.title: escape_string_warning
escape_string_warning.description:
Warn about backslash escapes in ordinary string literals.

Useful for providing warnings for interpreted-language applications which may be engaging in unsafe string escape behavior.
Unless you are currently porting or upgrading such an application, though, these warnings are not useful and should be turned off.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/escape_string_warning/
escape_string_warning.markdownDescription:
Warn about backslash escapes in ordinary string literals.

Useful for providing warnings for interpreted-language applications which may be engaging in unsafe string escape behavior.
Unless you are currently porting or upgrading such an application, though, these warnings are not useful and should be turned off.

see [escape_string_warning documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/escape_string_warning/)

event_source.title: event_source
event_source.description:
Sets the application name used to identify PostgreSQL messages in the event log.

Should be "postgres-1" or something else identifiable as this specific postmaster.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/event_source/
event_source.markdownDescription:
Sets the application name used to identify PostgreSQL messages in the event log.

Should be "postgres-1" or something else identifiable as this specific postmaster.

see [event_source documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/event_source/)

exit_on_error.title: exit_on_error
exit_on_error.description:
Terminate session on any error.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/exit_on_error/
exit_on_error.markdownDescription:
Terminate session on any error.

see [exit_on_error documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/exit_on_error/)

extension_destdir.title: extension_destdir
extension_destdir.description:
Path to prepend for extension loading

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/extension_destdir/
extension_destdir.markdownDescription:
Path to prepend for extension loading

see [extension_destdir documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/extension_destdir/)

external_pid_file.title: external_pid_file
external_pid_file.description:
Writes the postmaster PID to the specified file.

Creates an extra copy of the process ID.
Used for server administration tools which need a copy of the process ID in a specific directory.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/external_pid_file/
external_pid_file.markdownDescription:
Writes the postmaster PID to the specified file.

Creates an extra copy of the process ID.
Used for server administration tools which need a copy of the process ID in a specific directory.

see [external_pid_file documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/external_pid_file/)

extra_float_digits.title: extra_float_digits
extra_float_digits.description:
Sets the number of digits displayed for floating-point values.

Minimum: -15

Maximum: 3

Only significant for applications which do a lot of float calculations, like scientific databases.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/extra_float_digits/
extra_float_digits.markdownDescription:
Sets the number of digits displayed for floating-point values.

Minimum: -15

Maximum: 3

Only significant for applications which do a lot of float calculations, like scientific databases.

see [extra_float_digits documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/extra_float_digits/)

force_parallel_mode.title: force_parallel_mode
force_parallel_mode.description:
Forces use of parallel query facilities.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/force_parallel_mode/
force_parallel_mode.markdownDescription:
Forces use of parallel query facilities.

see [force_parallel_mode documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/force_parallel_mode/)

from_collapse_limit.title: from_collapse_limit
from_collapse_limit.description:
Sets the FROM-list size beyond which subqueries are not collapsed.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 2147483647

While it's probably true that newer CPUs could support higher collapse_limits, there's not much incremental benefit to just raising either collapse_limit to 10 or 11.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/from_collapse_limit/
from_collapse_limit.markdownDescription:
Sets the FROM-list size beyond which subqueries are not collapsed.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 2147483647

While it's probably true that newer CPUs could support higher collapse_limits, there's not much incremental benefit to just raising either collapse_limit to 10 or 11.

see [from_collapse_limit documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/from_collapse_limit/)

fsync.title: fsync
fsync.description:
Forces synchronization of updates to disk.

Never turn off unless your data is entirely disposable.
Setting fsync=off is the equivalent of saying " don't care about my data, I can recreate the database from scratch if I have to...  If synch activity is a performance concern, see synchronous_commit.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/fsync/
fsync.markdownDescription:
Forces synchronization of updates to disk.

Never turn off unless your data is entirely disposable.
Setting fsync=off is the equivalent of saying " don't care about my data, I can recreate the database from scratch if I have to...  If synch activity is a performance concern, see synchronous_commit.

see [fsync documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/fsync/)

full_page_writes.title: full_page_writes
full_page_writes.description:
Writes full pages to WAL when first modified after a checkpoint.

This is PostgreSQL's triple-check on transaction log integrity.
Leave it on unless you have enough in-depth knowledge of your filesystem and hardware to be certain that torn page writes of log segments are completely prevented.  Solaris/ZFS users claim to be able to turn this off, but that has not been destruction-tested.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/full_page_writes/
full_page_writes.markdownDescription:
Writes full pages to WAL when first modified after a checkpoint.

This is PostgreSQL's triple-check on transaction log integrity.
Leave it on unless you have enough in-depth knowledge of your filesystem and hardware to be certain that torn page writes of log segments are completely prevented.  Solaris/ZFS users claim to be able to turn this off, but that has not been destruction-tested.

see [full_page_writes documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/full_page_writes/)

geqo_effort.title: geqo_effort
geqo_effort.description:
GEQO: effort is used to set the default for other GEQO parameters.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 10

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_effort/
geqo_effort.markdownDescription:
GEQO: effort is used to set the default for other GEQO parameters.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 10

see [geqo_effort documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_effort/)

geqo_generations.title: geqo_generations
geqo_generations.description:
GEQO: number of iterations of the algorithm.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_generations/
geqo_generations.markdownDescription:
GEQO: number of iterations of the algorithm.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see [geqo_generations documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_generations/)

geqo_pool_size.title: geqo_pool_size
geqo_pool_size.description:
GEQO: number of individuals in the population.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_pool_size/
geqo_pool_size.markdownDescription:
GEQO: number of individuals in the population.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see [geqo_pool_size documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_pool_size/)

geqo_seed.title: geqo_seed
geqo_seed.description:
GEQO: seed for random path selection.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1

If you set this manually, you can force repeatable execution paths for GEQO queries.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_seed/
geqo_seed.markdownDescription:
GEQO: seed for random path selection.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 1

If you set this manually, you can force repeatable execution paths for GEQO queries.

see [geqo_seed documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_seed/)

geqo_selection_bias.title: geqo_selection_bias
geqo_selection_bias.description:
GEQO: selective pressure within the population.

Minimum: 1.5

Maximum: 2

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_selection_bias/
geqo_selection_bias.markdownDescription:
GEQO: selective pressure within the population.

Minimum: 1.5

Maximum: 2

see [geqo_selection_bias documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_selection_bias/)

geqo_threshold.title: geqo_threshold
geqo_threshold.description:
Sets the threshold of FROM items beyond which GEQO is used.

Minimum: 2

Maximum: 2147483647

With new, faster processors it's tempting to raise the geqo_threshold a little, such as to 16 or 18.
Increasing more than that is unwise as query planning time goes up geometrically.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_threshold/
geqo_threshold.markdownDescription:
Sets the threshold of FROM items beyond which GEQO is used.

Minimum: 2

Maximum: 2147483647

With new, faster processors it's tempting to raise the geqo_threshold a little, such as to 16 or 18.
Increasing more than that is unwise as query planning time goes up geometrically.

see [geqo_threshold documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/geqo_threshold/)

gin_fuzzy_search_limit.title: gin_fuzzy_search_limit
gin_fuzzy_search_limit.description:
Sets the maximum allowed result for exact search by GIN.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

If you're going to use GIN queries in a web application, it's generally useful to set a limit on how many rows can be returned from the index just for response times.
However, the maximum number needs to depend on your application; what do users see as an acceptable expression of "many"?

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/gin_fuzzy_search_limit/
gin_fuzzy_search_limit.markdownDescription:
Sets the maximum allowed result for exact search by GIN.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

If you're going to use GIN queries in a web application, it's generally useful to set a limit on how many rows can be returned from the index just for response times.
However, the maximum number needs to depend on your application; what do users see as an acceptable expression of "many"?

see [gin_fuzzy_search_limit documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/gin_fuzzy_search_limit/)

gin_pending_list_limit.title: gin_pending_list_limit
gin_pending_list_limit.description:
Sets the maximum size of the pending list for GIN index.

Minimum: 64

Maximum: 2147483647

Unless you have a lot of GIN indexed data and have time to test the performance of fastupdate.
Even then, it's probably better to set it on individual indexes.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/gin_pending_list_limit/
gin_pending_list_limit.markdownDescription:
Sets the maximum size of the pending list for GIN index.

Minimum: 64

Maximum: 2147483647

Unless you have a lot of GIN indexed data and have time to test the performance of fastupdate.
Even then, it's probably better to set it on individual indexes.

see [gin_pending_list_limit documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/gin_pending_list_limit/)

hash_mem_multiplier.title: hash_mem_multiplier
hash_mem_multiplier.description:
Multiple of work_mem to use for hash tables.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 1000

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/hash_mem_multiplier/
hash_mem_multiplier.markdownDescription:
Multiple of work_mem to use for hash tables.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 1000

see [hash_mem_multiplier documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/hash_mem_multiplier/)

hba_file.title: hba_file
hba_file.description:
Sets the server's "hba" configuration file.

Allows you to move the pg_hba file to a sysadmin-specified location.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/hba_file/
hba_file.markdownDescription:
Sets the server's "hba" configuration file.

Allows you to move the pg_hba file to a sysadmin-specified location.

see [hba_file documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/hba_file/)

hot_standby.title: hot_standby
hot_standby.description:
Allows connections and queries during recovery.

Set to "on", unless you want to specifically prohibit people from running queries on a standby server.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/hot_standby/
hot_standby.markdownDescription:
Allows connections and queries during recovery.

Set to "on", unless you want to specifically prohibit people from running queries on a standby server.

see [hot_standby documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/hot_standby/)

hot_standby_feedback.title: hot_standby_feedback
hot_standby_feedback.description:
Allows feedback from a hot standby to the primary that will avoid query conflicts.

Helps avoid query cancel on the replicas in most cases.
Turn it off for a replica which does long-running reports and is allowed to lag.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/hot_standby_feedback/
hot_standby_feedback.markdownDescription:
Allows feedback from a hot standby to the primary that will avoid query conflicts.

Helps avoid query cancel on the replicas in most cases.
Turn it off for a replica which does long-running reports and is allowed to lag.

see [hot_standby_feedback documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/hot_standby_feedback/)

huge_page_size.title: huge_page_size
huge_page_size.description:
The size of huge page that should be requested.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/huge_page_size/
huge_page_size.markdownDescription:
The size of huge page that should be requested.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see [huge_page_size documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/huge_page_size/)

huge_pages.title: huge_pages
huge_pages.description:
Use of huge pages on Linux or Windows.

However, for small systems (< 2GB of RAM) may be beneficial to set to "off".

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/huge_pages/
huge_pages.markdownDescription:
Use of huge pages on Linux or Windows.

However, for small systems (< 2GB of RAM) may be beneficial to set to "off".

see [huge_pages documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/huge_pages/)

ident_file.title: ident_file
ident_file.description:

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/ident_file/

see [ident_file documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/ident_file/)

idle_in_transaction_session_timeout.title: idle_in_transaction_session_timeout
idle_in_transaction_session_timeout.description:
Sets the maximum allowed idle time between queries, when in a transaction.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

Set to 1 hour maximum, or as low as 1 minute if you know your query load well.
Idle transactions are bad news.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/idle_in_transaction_session_timeout/
idle_in_transaction_session_timeout.markdownDescription:
Sets the maximum allowed idle time between queries, when in a transaction.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

Set to 1 hour maximum, or as low as 1 minute if you know your query load well.
Idle transactions are bad news.

see [idle_in_transaction_session_timeout documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/idle_in_transaction_session_timeout/)

idle_session_timeout.title: idle_session_timeout
idle_session_timeout.description:
Sets the maximum allowed idle time between queries, when not in a transaction.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/idle_session_timeout/
idle_session_timeout.markdownDescription:

ignore_checksum_failure.title: ignore_checksum_failure
ignore_checksum_failure.description:
Continues processing after a checksum failure.

For rescuing a corrupt DB

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/ignore_checksum_failure/
ignore_checksum_failure.markdownDescription:
Continues processing after a checksum failure.

For rescuing a corrupt DB

see [ignore_checksum_failure documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/ignore_checksum_failure/)

ignore_invalid_pages.title: ignore_invalid_pages
ignore_invalid_pages.description:
Continues recovery after an invalid pages failure.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/ignore_invalid_pages/
ignore_invalid_pages.markdownDescription:
Continues recovery after an invalid pages failure.

see [ignore_invalid_pages documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/ignore_invalid_pages/)

ignore_system_indexes.title: ignore_system_indexes
ignore_system_indexes.description:
Disables reading from system indexes.

Useful for salvaging data from a corrupted database.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/ignore_system_indexes/
ignore_system_indexes.markdownDescription:
Disables reading from system indexes.

see [ignore_system_indexes documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/ignore_system_indexes/)

in_hot_standby.title: in_hot_standby
in_hot_standby.description:

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/in_hot_standby/
in_hot_standby.markdownDescription:
Shows whether hot standby is currently active.

see [in_hot_standby documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/in_hot_standby/)

integer_datetimes.description:

Informational: lets you know of non-standard installation or compile options.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/integer_datetimes/
integer_datetimes.markdownDescription:
Shows whether datetimes are integer based.

This is just in case your applications are expecting something specific in how INTERVAL strings are output.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/IntervalStyle/
IntervalStyle.markdownDescription:
Sets the display format for interval values.

This is just in case your applications are expecting something specific in how INTERVAL strings are output.

see [IntervalStyle documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/IntervalStyle/)

jit_above_cost.title: jit_above_cost
jit_above_cost.description:

Maximum: 1.79769e+308

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/jit_above_cost/

jit_debugging_support.markdownDescription:

jit_profiling_support.markdownDescription:
Register JIT-compiled functions with perf profiler.

jit_tuple_deforming.description:

jit_tuple_deforming.markdownDescription:
Allow JIT compilation of tuple deforming.

see [jit_tuple_deforming documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/jit_tuple_deforming/)

join_collapse_limit.title: join_collapse_limit
join_collapse_limit.description:
Sets the FROM-list size beyond which JOIN constructs are not flattened.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 2147483647

If for some reason you wanted to explicitly declare the join order for all of your queries, you could set this to 1.
That is not recommended, though.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/join_collapse_limit/
join_collapse_limit.markdownDescription:
Sets the FROM-list size beyond which JOIN constructs are not flattened.

Minimum: 1

Maximum: 2147483647

If for some reason you wanted to explicitly declare the join order for all of your queries, you could set this to 1.
That is not recommended, though.

see [join_collapse_limit documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/join_collapse_limit/)

krb_caseins_users.title: krb_caseins_users
krb_caseins_users.description:
Sets whether Kerberos and GSSAPI user names should be treated as case-insensitive.

Speak with your sysadmin or network security about how to set the various kerberos settings to match your local kerberos setup.
Kerberos support must be compiled in to PostgreSQL, and set in pg_hba.conf.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/krb_caseins_users/
krb_caseins_users.markdownDescription:
Sets whether Kerberos and GSSAPI user names should be treated as case-insensitive.

Speak with your sysadmin or network security about how to set the various kerberos settings to match your local kerberos setup.
Kerberos support must be compiled in to PostgreSQL, and set in pg_hba.conf.

see [krb_caseins_users documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/krb_caseins_users/)

krb_server_keyfile.title: krb_server_keyfile
krb_server_keyfile.description:
Sets the location of the Kerberos server key file.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/krb_server_keyfile/

lock_timeout.title: lock_timeout
lock_timeout.description:
Sets the maximum allowed duration of any wait for a lock.

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 2147483647

... but consider setting this per application or per query for any explicit locking attempts.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/lock_timeout/

log_autovacuum_min_duration.title: log_autovacuum_min_duration
log_autovacuum_min_duration.description:
Sets the minimum execution time above which autovacuum actions will be logged.

Minimum: -1

Maximum: 2147483647

Logs all autovacuum actions which take more than the specified time.
Useful for figuring out if autovacuum is bogging down your system or blocking.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_autovacuum_min_duration/

see [log_autovacuum_min_duration documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_autovacuum_min_duration/)

log_checkpoints.title: log_checkpoints
log_checkpoints.description:
Logs each checkpoint.

When doing performance analysis, it's often a good idea to turn on most of the logging options and log them to a CSVlog.


see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_checkpoints/

Logs each checkpoint.

log_connections.description:

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_connections/
log_connections.markdownDescription:
Logs each successful connection.

Useful for performance analysis.

see [log_connections documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_connections/)

log_destination.title: log_destination
log_destination.description:
Sets the destination for server log output.

Your choice of log destination depends on your system administration plans and the status of your server.
"syslog" or "eventlog" (Windows) are good choices for most development servers, because they can support centralized log monitors.  For development and testing, however, "csvlog" is probably the most useful, as it allows you to run queries against the log contents.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_destination/
log_destination.markdownDescription:
Sets the destination for server log output.

Your choice of log destination depends on your system administration plans and the status of your server.
"syslog" or "eventlog" (Windows) are good choices for most development servers, because they can support centralized log monitors.  For development and testing, however, "csvlog" is probably the most useful, as it allows you to run queries against the log contents.

see [log_destination documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_destination/)

log_directory.title: log_directory
log_directory.description:
Sets the destination directory for log files.

If you are having PostgreSQL keep its own activity logs on a production server, it's probably a good idea to locate them on separate storage from the database and transaction log.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_directory/
log_directory.markdownDescription:
Sets the destination directory for log files.

If you are having PostgreSQL keep its own activity logs on a production server, it's probably a good idea to locate them on separate storage from the database and transaction log.

see [log_directory documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_directory/)

log_disconnections.title: log_disconnections
log_disconnections.description:

Useful for performance analysis.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_disconnections/
log_disconnections.markdownDescription:

log_error_verbosity.description:
Sets the verbosity of logged messages.

Unless doing intensive debugging.
Alternately, set to TERSE if managing log volume is becoming a problem.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_error_verbosity/

log_file_mode.title: log_file_mode
log_file_mode.description:

Minimum: 0

Maximum: 511

... unless you need to share the log with Postgres' unix group, in which case set it to 660.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_file_mode/

see [log_file_mode documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_file_mode/)

log_filename.title: log_filename
log_filename.description:
Sets the file name pattern for log files.

If you want your logs to rotate automatically without needing a cron job to delete old logs, try naming them after the days of the week or the month so they overwrite automatically (i.e. 'postgresql-%a' or 'postgresql-%d').
This also helps with log analysis.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_filename/
log_filename.markdownDescription:
Sets the file name pattern for log files.

If you want your logs to rotate automatically without needing a cron job to delete old logs, try naming them after the days of the week or the month so they overwrite automatically (i.e. 'postgresql-%a' or 'postgresql-%d').
This also helps with log analysis.

see [log_filename documentation](https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_filename/)

log_hostname.title: log_hostname
log_hostname.description:
Logs the host name in the connection logs.

As this setting requires resolution of each connecting hostname, it's pretty much always too expensive to have on, even when troubleshooting.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_hostname/
log_hostname.markdownDescription:

log_line_prefix.title: log_line_prefix
log_line_prefix.description:
Controls information prefixed to each log line.

Primarily useful for providing extra information when logging to syslog or eventlog.
Try "%h:%d:%u:%c  %t" for this.

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_line_prefix/
log_line_prefix.markdownDescription:

log_lock_waits.markdownDescription:

log_min_duration_sample.description:

Minimum: -1

Maximum: 2147483647

see https://postgresqlco.nf/doc/en/param/log_min_duration_sample/
log_min_duration_sample.markdownDescription:
Sets the minimum execution time above which a sample of statements will be logged. Sampling is determined by log_statement_sample_rate.
